Three years.
Dozens of broken
workflows.
One fix at a time.

I'm Shounak. I spent three years inside Dassault Falcon Jet's digital backbone. Then I went back to school to understand why the hard parts are always the human parts.

About

Thoughtful.
Curious.
Calm under
complexity.

Systems minded
Data reveals direction
Empathy keeps it grounded
Always learning. Always building.

I didn't start in product design. I started in a warehouse, debugging a barcode system that nobody had touched in a decade. It crashed. I fixed it. Then I rebuilt it. That pattern kept repeating.

At Dassault Falcon Jet, I worked on systems that handled private jet logistics, customer communications, and inventory for a global fleet. When the data was wrong, people noticed. When the process was slow, people felt it.

What I learned: friction has a cost. Not just in hours, but in trust. And removing it is as much a design problem as it is a technical one.

Now I'm at Rutgers studying product design, asking the next set of questions. How do you build something people actually want to use? What does "good" feel like when there's no manual to fall back on?

Selected Work
01
Power BI Command Center

Executives were making decisions with data that was three days old. I built a live dashboard integrating QlikSense and internal systems into wall-mounted Command Center screens. Manual reporting dropped by more than 30%. The data stopped being an afterthought and became the first thing people checked each morning.

30%+ reduction in manual reporting
Power BI QlikSense Data Visualization
02
SAP Warehouse Automation

A warehouse team was spending 52 hours a year typing the same information into a barcode system. That's more than a full work week, every year, for a task a script could handle in milliseconds. I built that script. One click. Done. The team used those hours on work that actually needed a human.

52 hours returned annually
SAP VBS Scripting Power Automate
03
Legacy Receipt System Rebuild

A 20-year-old receipt system was quietly producing wrong numbers. The errors were small enough to miss, but big enough to matter across 75,000 records. I rebuilt it in Microsoft Access with automated billing logic and receipt generation. The errors stopped. The processing time dropped. Nobody had to manually check the math again.

75,000+ records, zero recurring errors
MS Access Systems Architecture Automation
04
AI Flight Report Router

Twenty customer flight reports landed every day. Someone had to read each one, figure out where it belonged, and forward it. That someone was a person spending real time on triage that felt like it should be automatic. I built an AI-integrated Power Automate flow to classify and route them. That person got their time back.

20+ daily reports auto-routed
AI Integration Power Automate Classification
05
Global Power Platform Rollout

250 employees across four continents needed to be ready for new tools. I ran the training, validated the pilot environments, and coordinated the rollout across the US, Europe, Australia, and Asia. Zero downtime. That kind of outcome only happens when you test everything twice and plan for the thing that wasn't in the plan.

250 trained, 4 continents, 0 downtime
Power Platform Change Management Global Deployment
